content:
Configure your Next.js app to behave differently in development and production without scattering hardcoded values across your codebase. Learn a clean, scalable approach to environment based configuration that works in real world projects.

3 min read
3 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

How to Add Environment-Based Config in Next.jsIf you’ve ever pushed a Next.js app to production only to realize it’s still calling your local API, you’re not alone. It’s one of those mistakes that usually happens once, and then you become very careful about configuration.
As projects grow, different environments need different settings. Your development environment might use a local database and verbose logging, while production connects to live services and keeps logging to a minimum.
The good news is that Next.js has excellent support for environment variables. Once you understand how to organize them, switching between development and production becomes effortless.
In this article, we’ll build a simple mental model for environment based configuration and look at practical patterns you can use in production.
Why Environment Based Configuration Matters
